title: HashiCorp Vault Secrets Management Checklist
description: Set up and run HashiCorp Vault in production: HA storage, TLS, auto-unseal, dynamic secrets, encryption as a service, and the policies, audit, and backups that keep it safe.

items.title: Run a real server config with Integrated Storage, never dev mode
items.description: Dev mode runs in memory, starts unsealed, and prints a root token to the screen. It drops every secret on restart. Run a proper server config backed by Integrated Storage (Raft) so you get an HA cluster and encrypted state on disk.

codeBlocks.label: /etc/vault.d/vault.hcl
Start the server (and what NOT to run)
codeBlocks.code:
# Production: a config file, a 3 or 5 node Raft cluster.
vault server -config=/etc/vault.d/vault.hcl

# NEVER in production. In-memory, auto-unsealed, root token on stdout:
# vault server -dev

items.title: Enable TLS on every listener
items.description: With TLS off, every token and secret crosses the network in plaintext. Anyone sniffing the wire gets your credentials. Keep tls_disable off and point the listener at a real certificate, not a self-signed throwaway.

codeBlocks.label: TLS listener block
codeBlocks.code:
listener "tcp" {
  address         = "0.0.0.0:8200"
  tls_disable     = false
  tls_cert_file   = "/etc/vault.d/tls/vault.crt"
  tls_key_file    = "/etc/vault.d/tls/vault.key"
  tls_min_version = "tls12"
}

codeBlocks.label: Verify TLS is actually on
codeBlocks.code:
export VAULT_ADDR=https://vault.internal:8200

# Should connect over https and show the seal/HA status:
vault status

# Health endpoint should serve over TLS, not plain http:
curl -s https://vault.internal:8200/v1/sys/health | jq .

items.title: Auto-unseal with a cloud KMS and split the recovery keys
items.description: A sealed Vault answers nothing until someone enters unseal keys. Manual Shamir unseal means every restart needs humans on a call. Use auto-unseal with a KMS, and split the recovery keys across several people so no single person can rekey or take over the cluster.

codeBlocks.label: Auto-unseal with AWS KMS
Initialize with split recovery keys
codeBlocks.code:
# 5 recovery shares, any 3 needed to rekey. PGP-encrypt each share
# so it lands in a specific person's inbox, not in plaintext.
vault operator init \
  -recovery-shares=5 \
  -recovery-threshold=3 \
  -recovery-pgp-keys="alice.asc,bob.asc,carol.asc,dave.asc,erin.asc"

items.title: Revoke the initial root token after bootstrap
items.description: The initial root token has unlimited access and never expires. Leaving it in a CI variable or on a laptop is a standing breach. Revoke it once auth methods and policies are in place, and generate a fresh one only when you actually need root.

codeBlocks.label: Revoke root and regenerate on demand
codeBlocks.code:
# After setting up auth methods + policies, kill the bootstrap token:
vault token revoke <initial-root-token>

# Need root later? Generate a one-time root token. Key holders
# each supply a recovery key to authorize it:
vault operator generate-root -init
vault operator generate-root -nonce=<nonce>   # run per key holder

items.title: Enable audit devices before any real traffic
items.description: Without an audit device you have no record of who read which secret. Turn one on before workloads connect. Run two devices, because if every audit device fails to write, Vault stops answering requests by design, so a single log disk filling up takes you down.

codeBlocks.label: Enable file and syslog audit devices
codeBlocks.code:
# Primary: a file device (secrets are HMAC'd, not stored in clear):
vault audit enable file file_path=/var/log/vault/audit.log

# Secondary, so one failing device does not block Vault:
vault audit enable -path=syslog syslog tag="vault" facility="AUTH"

# Confirm what is enabled:
vault audit list -detailed

items.title: Use auth methods instead of handing out tokens
items.description: Distributing long-lived tokens is the same mistake as sharing passwords. Use an auth method so each app or person proves identity and gets a short-lived token scoped to one policy. AppRole for machines, Kubernetes auth for pods, OIDC for humans.

codeBlocks.label: AppRole for a service
codeBlocks.code:
vault auth enable approle

vault write auth/approle/role/myapp \
  token_policies="myapp" \
  token_ttl=20m \
  token_max_ttl=1h \
  secret_id_ttl=10m \
  secret_id_num_uses=1

codeBlocks.label: Kubernetes auth for pods
codeBlocks.code:
vault auth enable kubernetes

vault write auth/kubernetes/config \
  kubernetes_host="https://$KUBERNETES_PORT_443_TCP_ADDR:443"

vault write auth/kubernetes/role/myapp \
  bound_service_account_names=myapp \
  bound_service_account_namespaces=default \
  policies=myapp \
  ttl=20m

items.title: Write least-privilege policies and never attach root to apps
items.description: Vault denies everything by default, so a path you do not grant is a path nobody can touch. Write narrow policies that allow read on exactly the paths an app needs. Attaching the root policy to a workload throws that whole model away.

codeBlocks.label: myapp-policy.hcl
Apply and test the policy
codeBlocks.code:
vault policy write myapp myapp-policy.hcl

# Sanity check: a token with this policy can read its path...
vault read secret/data/myapp/db
# ...and gets a 403 on anything outside it:
vault read secret/data/other-team/api  # permission denied

items.title: Use dynamic secrets instead of static database passwords
items.description: Static database passwords live forever and leak quietly into config files and dumps. The database secrets engine creates a unique user per request with a short TTL and deletes it when the lease ends. A credential that leaks is useless within the hour.

codeBlocks.label: Configure the Postgres database engine
codeBlocks.code:
vault secrets enable database

vault write database/config/app-postgres \
  plugin_name=postgresql-database-plugin \
  allowed_roles="myapp-ro" \
  connection_url="postgresql://{{username}}:{{password}}@db.internal:5432/app?sslmode=require" \
  username="vault-admin" \
  password="$ADMIN_PW"

vault write database/roles/myapp-ro \
  db_name=app-postgres \
  creation_statements="CREATE ROLE \"{{name}}\" WITH LOGIN PASSWORD '{{password}}' VALID UNTIL '{{expiration}}'; GRANT SELECT ON ALL TABLES IN SCHEMA public TO \"{{name}}\";" \
  default_ttl="1h" \
  max_ttl="24h"

codeBlocks.label: Read a short-lived credential
codeBlocks.code:
vault read database/creds/myapp-ro
# Key                Value
# ---                -----
# lease_id           database/creds/myapp-ro/AbCd1234...
# lease_duration     1h
# password           A1a-9x8y7z...
# username           v-approle-myapp-ro-8s7d6f5g

items.title: Keep TTLs short and know the revoke commands
items.description: Long leases mean a stolen token still works days later. Keep default and max lease TTLs short on every mount, and learn the revoke commands before you need them. During an incident you want to kill every credential under a path with one command.

codeBlocks.label: Tune TTLs and revoke leases
codeBlocks.code:
# Cap how long leases on the database mount can live:
vault secrets tune -default-lease-ttl=1h -max-lease-ttl=24h database/

# Revoke one lease:
vault lease revoke database/creds/myapp-ro/AbCd1234...

# Incident response: revoke every lease under a prefix at once:
vault lease revoke -prefix database/creds/

items.title: Encrypt with the transit engine instead of shipping keys to apps
items.description: Do not copy encryption keys onto your app servers; one compromised host and the keys walk out with it. The transit engine encrypts and decrypts for you, so the key never leaves Vault. Your app sends plaintext, gets ciphertext back, and stores only the ciphertext.

codeBlocks.label: Encrypt and decrypt without holding the key
codeBlocks.code:
vault secrets enable transit
vault write -f transit/keys/orders

# Encrypt (plaintext must be base64):
vault write transit/encrypt/orders \
  plaintext=$(echo -n "4111-1111-1111-1111" | base64)
# ciphertext  vault:v1:8SDd3WHDOjf7mq69CyCqYjBXAiQQAVZRkFM13ok481zoCmHnSeDX9vyf7w==

# Decrypt:
vault write transit/decrypt/orders \
  ciphertext="vault:v1:8SDd3WHDOjf7mq69CyCqYjBXAiQQAVZRkFM13ok481zoCmHnSeDX9vyf7w=="

items.title: Rotate encryption keys on a schedule
items.description: Keys that never rotate turn one leak into a permanent one. Rotate transit keys regularly; old ciphertext still decrypts against earlier versions, and rewrap moves stored data onto the newest key. Rotate Vault's own underlying encryption key too.

codeBlocks.label: Rotate transit keys and rewrap data
codeBlocks.code:
# New key version. Old ciphertext (vault:v1:...) still decrypts:
vault write -f transit/keys/orders/rotate

# Move existing ciphertext onto the latest version:
vault write transit/rewrap/orders \
  ciphertext="vault:v1:8SDd3WHDOjf7mq69CyCqYjBXAiQQ..."

# Rotate Vault's own encryption key (new key for future writes):
vault operator rotate

items.title: Deliver secrets with response wrapping
items.description: Passing a secret_id or token through a CI log or an env dump exposes it to anyone who reads the log. Response wrapping returns a single-use token that the receiver unwraps once. If someone intercepts it, the unwrap fails and you know the secret was seen.

codeBlocks.label: Wrap a secret_id and unwrap it once
codeBlocks.code:
# CI or an operator generates a wrapped secret_id (valid 120s):
vault write -wrap-ttl=120s -f auth/approle/role/myapp/secret-id
# wrapping_token:  hvs.CAESIJ...

# The app unwraps exactly once to get the real value:
VAULT_TOKEN=hvs.CAESIJ... vault unwrap
# A second unwrap fails, which tells you the token was intercepted.

items.title: Automate Raft snapshots and test the restore
items.description: If the Raft data is lost you lose every secret, policy, and mount. Take automated snapshots, store them offsite and encrypted, and actually run a restore on a throwaway cluster. A backup you have never restored is just a guess.

codeBlocks.label: Snapshot, restore, and schedule it
codeBlocks.code:
# Take a snapshot (run against the active node):
vault operator raft snapshot save vault-$(date +%F).snap

# Restore into a running cluster (test this, do not assume):
vault operator raft snapshot restore vault-2026-06-26.snap

# Automate with a systemd timer or cron, then ship the file
# to encrypted offsite storage:
# 0 */6 * * * vault operator raft snapshot save /backup/vault-$(date +\%F-\%H).snap

items.title: Export telemetry and alert on seal status
items.description: A Vault that has resealed or lost quorum fails every request, and you want to hear that from an alert, not from a wall of app errors. Export telemetry to Prometheus and alert on seal status, lease and token counts, and audit write failures.

codeBlocks.label: Telemetry block
Alert on seal status
codeBlocks.code:
# Health endpoint returns 503 when sealed or in standby.
# Sealed = true is your highest-priority page:
curl -s https://vault.internal:8200/v1/sys/health | jq '{sealed, standby}'

# Key Prometheus metrics to alert on:
#   vault.core.unsealed == 0        (Vault is sealed)
#   vault.expire.num_leases  spike   (lease leak / runaway client)
#   vault.audit.log_request_failure  (audit device failing)
